nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or standing waters with leaf litter, submerged roots, and dense cover; rivers, floodplains, and forest streams with sand or fine gravel substrate and abundant hiding places

decorPreferences: caves and PVC tube hides sized to fit adult body
smooth or fine sand substrate (protects ventral scutes)
dense planting or floating plants to dim lighting
leaf litter (Indian almond or oak leaves) for naturalistic biotope

diet.notes: Opportunistic omnivore with a strong lean toward invertebrates and bottom-dwelling prey. Readily consumes sinking pellets, catfish wafers, shrimp pellets, and algae wafers. Relishes frozen or live foods: bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, earthworms, and small snails. Will crush and consume snail shells — a useful natural pest controller. Small crustaceans (dwarf shrimp) may be eaten. Offer food after lights-out to suit nocturnal feeding behaviour.

goodWith: medium to large tetras (e.g. Congo tetra, bleeding heart)
peaceful cichlids (e.g. angelfish, discus, keyhole cichlid)
corydoras and other peaceful catfish (avoid very small species as tank-mates)
larger barbs (e.g. tiger barb groups, denison barb)
avoidWith: dwarf shrimp (cherry shrimp, crystal shrimp — will be eaten)
small snails intended to be kept (will be consumed)
aggressive cichlids that may attack a slow-moving bottom feeder
very small fish under ~3 cm that could be inadvertently swallowed
fine-net handling without thick gloves (pectoral spines lock erect and pierce nets)

summary: A robust, long-lived Amazonian armored catfish prized for its bold white-on-dark lateral stripe and charismatic nocturnal personality. Hardy and easy to keep; tolerates a wide range of water conditions. Notable for vocalizing via pectoral stridulation and for locking its pectoral spines erect when stressed — a defence mechanism that can pierce nets and injure handlers.
careNotes:
The Striped Raphael Catfish (Platydoras armatulus) is one of the most durable and rewarding bottom-dwellers in the freshwater hobby. Adults reach 18–20 cm and require a tank of at least 200 L with a 120 cm footprint; the long lifespan of 15–20 years demands long-term commitment. A secure-fitting lid is advisable — although escape risk is moderate rather than high, large catfish can push lids under cover of darkness.

This species is strictly nocturnal. Daytime retreats into caves, PVC pipes, or under driftwood are essential; a fish denied adequate hides will remain hidden and stressed. Feeding should be done at lights-out or under dim red lighting to encourage natural foraging. Fine sand or smooth fine gravel is preferred as substrate; coarse gravel can abrade ventral scutes.

The common name 'Talking Catfish' reflects a genuine acoustic ability: the fish produces audible clicking or grunting sounds by rubbing the pectoral spine base against the pectoral girdle (pectoral stridulation), typically when netted or otherwise stressed. This same mechanism produces the locked pectoral spine — handlers should use thick wet gloves and never use fine-mesh nets; a spine can perforate skin and become embedded.

Taxonomic note: Platydoras armatulus is the species almost universally traded under the 'Striped Raphael' name. It was long confused with P. costatus, which is now considered a separate, more restricted species. Confirm scientific name when sourcing.

Maintain good water quality; although P. armatulus tolerates a wide pH and hardness range, ammonia spikes are poorly tolerated — a common cause of loss in newly set-up tanks. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Copper-based medications should be used with care around all scaleless and armored catfishes. Water temperature 22–28 °C suits most community tank companions.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Platydoras armatulus has been reported but is extremely rare in hobby settings. No reliable protocol exists for home aquariums. Spawning behaviour in the wild is not well documented; the species is thought to be a seasonal batch spawner triggered by flooding and temperature shifts. Sexing adults is difficult externally. Commercial stock is wild-caught or farm-raised in large facilities in South America and Southeast Asia. Not realistically achievable for the typical hobbyist.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Platydoras armatulus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~20 cm), temperature range (22–28°C), pH (6.0–7.8), hardness, native range (Amazon basin: Brazil, Bolivia, Peru, Colombia, Ecuador), habitat notes, diet, biotope, taxonomy (Doradidae, Siluriformes), nocturnal behaviour, pectoral spine locking mechanism, stridulation vocalization, and taxonomic note clarifying armatulus vs. costatus. Life expectancy 15–20 years cited.

additional.name: FishBase — Platydoras armatulus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 20.0 cm SL), temperature tolerance, native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), taxonomy (Siluriformes: Doradidae), diet classification (invertivore/omnivore), and depth range. Confirmed species is distinct from P. costatus per current taxonomy.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Striped Raphael Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-hobby perspective. Confirmed easy care level, minimum 55-gallon (208 L) tank, sinking-food diet preferences, nocturnal activity, long lifespan (10–20 years), snail-eating behaviour, pectoral spine hazard for handlers, and community compatibility notes. Temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.5–7.5.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Doradid catfish
additional.notes: Background on Doradidae family husbandry: armored scutes, pectoral spine locking mechanism and injury risk to handlers, stridulation as a genus-wide trait, nocturnal foraging, cave and driftwood requirements. Supplemented handling-safety and careNotes detail.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Platydoras armatulus profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist profile article covering origin (Amazon basin), adult size, the P. armatulus vs P. costatus taxonomic revision, aquarium setup recommendations (caves, sand substrate, low light), diet (omnivore leaning to invertebrates and snails), long captive lifespan, and vocalization behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported only in exceptional circumstances; no reliable home-aquarium protocol exists. Wild-caught or commercially farm-raised. Sexing adults externally is difficult. Not a realistic breeding species for the typical hobbyist.
